title: BFS & DFS
analogy: BFS is like ripples spreading outward from a stone thrown in water — it explores all neighbors at the same distance first. DFS is like following a single path deep into a forest before backtracking.

overview.summary: Breadth-First Search (BFS) and Depth-First Search (DFS) are the two fundamental strategies to visit every node in a graph or tree.
learningObjectives: Understand when to use BFS vs DFS.
Implement both traversals using queues and stacks.
Recognize BFS for shortest-path and DFS for exhaustive exploration.
overview.interviewRelevance: Together, BFS and DFS form the backbone of 30%+ of graph and tree interview problems.

paragraphs: Google Maps uses BFS (Dijkstra's variant) to find shortest routes. Facebook's friend-suggestion algorithm uses BFS to find 'friends of friends' layer by layer.
File system search (find command) uses DFS to recursively descend into directories before exploring siblings.
realWorldHook.ahaMoment: BFS guarantees the shortest path in unweighted graphs because it explores all nodes at distance d before any node at distance d+1.

definition.technicalDefinition: BFS uses a queue to explore nodes layer-by-layer. DFS uses a stack (or recursion) to explore as deep as possible before backtracking.

vocabulary.definition: Breadth-First Search — explores neighbors before going deeper.
Depth-First Search — explores as deep as possible before backtracking.
A set tracking already-explored nodes to prevent infinite loops.
In BFS, all nodes at the same distance from the source.

keyProperties: BFS finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
DFS is memory-efficient for deep graphs (stack depth vs queue breadth).
Both visit every reachable node exactly once: O(V + E).

operations.name: BFS Traversal
operations.description: Start at a node, enqueue it, then repeatedly dequeue and enqueue unvisited neighbors.
operations.visualNote: Nodes light up in concentric rings radiating from the start node.

operations.name: DFS Traversal
operations.description: Start at a node, recursively visit unvisited neighbors, going as deep as possible.
operations.visualNote: A single path lights up going deep, then backtracks and explores another branch.

practice.title: Number of Islands
practice.keyInsight: Start BFS/DFS from each unvisited land cell.

practice.title: Clone Graph
practice.keyInsight: BFS/DFS with a hash map of original → clone.

practice.title: Course Schedule
practice.keyInsight: Topological sort via DFS cycle detection.

practice.title: Word Ladder
practice.keyInsight: BFS on a word-graph where edges connect words differing by one letter.

practice.title: Surrounded Regions
practice.keyInsight: DFS from border O's to mark safe regions.

conceptQuiz.question: Which traversal guarantees the shortest path in an unweighted graph?
conceptQuiz.explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level, so the first time it reaches a node, it's via the shortest path.

conceptQuiz.question: What data structure does BFS primarily use?
conceptQuiz.explanation: BFS processes nodes in FIFO order using a queue to maintain the exploration frontier.

recognitionQuiz.problemStatement: Find the minimum number of steps to reach from cell (0,0) to cell (m-1,n-1) in a grid, moving in 4 directions.
recognitionQuiz.explanation: Shortest path in an unweighted grid is a classic BFS problem.
